title: Comments and character references

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Comments and character references; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Comments and character references
sources.claim: HTML comments annotate source without becoming rendered text, while character references represent characters that would otherwise be syntax or hard to type.

canonicalExplanation.summary: HTML comments annotate source without becoming rendered text, while character references represent characters that would otherwise be syntax or hard to type.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The source writes <code> to display literal angle brackets instead of creating a code element. Your goal is to repair text that is accidentally parsed as markup and remove misleading commented-out secrets. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Elements, attributes and nesting. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: HTML comments annotate source without becoming rendered text, while character references represent characters that would otherwise be syntax or hard to type. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Comments are visible to anyone receiving source and must never contain secrets; unnecessary entities can reduce readability. This draft therefore rejects: HTML comments are private and safe for API keys because browsers do not display them.

includes: Use comments for maintainers and encode reserved characters without using entities unnecessarily.
Repair text that is accidentally parsed as markup and remove misleading commented-out secrets.
excludes: Comments are visible to anyone receiving source and must never contain secrets; unnecessary entities can reduce readability.

outcomes: Repair text that is accidentally parsed as markup and remove misleading commented-out secrets.

examples.title: Comments and character references: concrete case
examples.situation: The source writes <code> to display literal angle brackets instead of creating a code element.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: The source writes <code> to display literal angle brackets instead of creating a code element.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Comments remain in delivered source and are not private; use character references only when needed to represent data safely.
examples.result: The result follows because Comments remain in delivered source and are not private; use character references only when needed to represent data safely.

examples.title: Comments and character references: boundary case

Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: HTML comments are private and safe for API keys because browsers do not display them.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Comments remain in delivered source and are not private; use character references only when needed to represent data safely.
